title: Quiz: Native Gate Implementations
description: Test your understanding of quantum gate implementations in neutral atom systems

questions.question: Why are hyperfine clock states (mF=0) preferred for qubit encoding in neutral atoms?

options.text: They have the longest radiative lifetime
They are insensitive to magnetic field fluctuations to first order
They have the strongest coupling to Rydberg states
They are easier to image with fluorescence

questions.explanation: Clock states (mF=0) are first-order insensitive to magnetic field variations. Their energy shift with magnetic field is quadratic rather than linear, providing inherent protection against magnetic noise and longer coherence times.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Raman transitions over microwave transitions for single-qubit gates?

options.text: Raman transitions have no spontaneous emission
Raman transitions can be locally addressed with focused beams
Raman transitions are always faster than microwave
Raman transitions don't require lasers

questions.explanation: Raman transitions use optical photons that can be tightly focused, enabling local addressing of individual atoms. Microwave fields cannot be focused to sub-wavelength scales, so they address all atoms globally.

questions.question: In the standard sequential CZ gate protocol, what happens during step 2 when both atoms started in |1>?

options.text: Atom 2 completes a full 2pi rotation to Rydberg and back
Atom 2's transition is blocked because atom 1 is in Rydberg
Both atoms are excited to Rydberg simultaneously
Atom 2 remains unaffected regardless of atom 1's state

questions.explanation: In step 2, atom 1 is already in the Rydberg state. Due to blockade, atom 2 cannot be excited to Rydberg - the strong interaction shifts the |rr> state out of resonance. This blocking is what creates the conditional phase on |11>.

questions.question: How can a CNOT gate be constructed from native neutral atom operations?

options.text: CNOT is a native gate that requires no decomposition
CNOT = CZ (CNOT and CZ are equivalent)
CNOT = H_target * CZ * H_target
CNOT = CZ * CZ (two CZ gates in sequence)

questions.explanation: CNOT can be constructed as Hadamard on target, followed by CZ, followed by Hadamard on target: CNOT = H_2 * CZ * H_2. This is because CZ creates a phase flip on |11>, and the Hadamards convert this to a bit flip on the target conditioned on the control.

questions.question: What is a composite pulse in the context of quantum gates?

options.text: A pulse applied to multiple atoms simultaneously
A sequence of pulses designed to be robust against systematic errors
A pulse with multiple frequency components
A pulse that implements multiple gates at once

questions.explanation: Composite pulses are carefully designed sequences of pulses that achieve the desired rotation while being robust against systematic errors like pulse amplitude or frequency miscalibration. Examples include BB1 and CORPSE sequences.

questions.question: Adiabatic pulse shaping (gradual turn-on/off) helps reduce leakage to non-computational states during Rydberg gates.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. Sudden (diabatic) pulse edges can excite transitions to unwanted states. Smooth, adiabatic pulse envelopes keep the system in the desired dressed states, reducing leakage to non-computational levels like higher Rydberg states or motional excitations.

questions.question: Which of the following are typical error sources for two-qubit Rydberg gates? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Spontaneous emission from the Rydberg state
Finite blockade strength causing |rr> leakage
Doppler shifts from atomic motion
Cosmic ray interference

questions.explanation: The main error sources are (a) spontaneous emission during the gate time, (b) imperfect blockade allowing some |rr> population, and (c) Doppler shifts from thermal atomic motion. Cosmic rays (d) are not a significant error source for neutral atom gates.
